winners.rationale: Reserve diversification keeps a steady bid; buyers sit on appreciating holdings.
ETF issuers and bullion banks gain on inflows and elevated turnover.

losers.rationale: Electronics and dental buyers face higher input costs with no substitute.

winners.rationale: Sustained high prices lift miner margins and cash flow.
Continued accumulation looks validated if the monetary bid holds.

losers.rationale: Industrial users keep absorbing cost; some demand destruction at the margin.

winners.rationale: If de-dollarisation persists, reserve gold is a long-horizon store of value.

losers.rationale: Tight, well-arbitraged markets compress the spreads traders live on.

winners.rationale: Rides gold's monetary bid; ETF flows amplify the move.
Primary silver miners gain operating leverage on a rising price.

losers.rationale: Solar and electronics manufacturers face a rising, volatile input cost.

winners.rationale: Industrial (solar) demand plus the monetary bid supports producer revenue.

losers.rationale: Solar makers pressured to thrift silver per cell or substitute.

winners.rationale: Thrifting and substitution R&D pays off; cost exposure falls.

losers.rationale: Per-unit industrial demand erodes as buyers design silver out.

winners.rationale: Producer discipline and a firm price support South African miner margins.
Producing states benefit from steadier export revenue.

losers.rationale: Autocatalyst buyers face cost pressure amid supply restructuring.

winners.rationale: Hydrogen fuel-cell and electrolyzer makers gain as platinum demand pivots toward them.

losers.rationale: Diesel-autocatalyst demand keeps shrinking; volume base narrows.

winners.rationale: If hydrogen scales, platinum's new demand pillar favours fuel-cell integrators.

losers.rationale: Structural EV substitution erodes the legacy autocatalyst market the majors depend on.
Producing states face a long decline in a core export unless hydrogen offsets it.

winners.rationale: Volatility in a thin market creates trading spreads.

losers.rationale: Russia and South Africa face soft export revenue on a weak price.
Producer margins squeezed as the price stays pressured.

winners.rationale: Gasoline-autocatalyst buyers benefit from cheaper, well-supplied palladium.

losers.rationale: Continued EV substitution keeps demand and price under pressure.

winners.rationale: Remaining ICE producers enjoy a structurally cheap input.

losers.rationale: Palladium is the most EV-exposed PGM; long-run demand decline hits producers hardest.
A core export erodes with little hydrogen-style offset.

winners.rationale: Extreme thinness makes rhodium a trader's market on any volatility.

losers.rationale: Autocatalyst makers exposed to sharp, unpredictable price spikes.

winners.rationale: Tight supply can spike the price, rewarding the few producers.

losers.rationale: No substitute for rhodium's emissions role; buyers bear the volatility.

winners.rationale: EV transition eventually shrinks the autocatalyst need that drives rhodium risk.

losers.rationale: The same transition removes rhodium's near-sole demand source.
A shrinking market offers less volume to trade.

winners.rationale: China's permitting cuts tighten supply, supporting price for those still producing.
Low-cost majors gain as high-cost supply exits.

losers.rationale: Battery makers face a firming input cost after the long price slide.

winners.rationale: As the surplus closes, surviving producers regain pricing power.

losers.rationale: A rebalancing market narrows the dislocations traders exploited.

winners.rationale: Vertically integrated battery makers with locked offtake secure supply on their terms.
Structural EV/storage demand growth rewards established producers.

losers.rationale: New entrants (Africa, the Americas) erode any single state's leverage over time.

winners.rationale: Glencore-style traders profit from moving a concentrated, opaque supply.

losers.rationale: Oversupply keeps the price soft, pressuring producer margins.

winners.rationale: Battery makers benefit from cheap, well-supplied cobalt — and from chemistries using less of it.

losers.rationale: DRC sees soft revenue from a price-pressured core export.

winners.rationale: Low-/no-cobalt cathode chemistries reduce buyers' exposure entirely.

losers.rationale: Thrifting and substitution shrink the addressable market.
The DRC's concentration advantage matters less as demand-per-battery falls.

winners.rationale: Indonesia's low-cost supply lets it gain share even on a weak price.

losers.rationale: Higher-cost producers elsewhere are squeezed below the cost line.

winners.rationale: Stainless and battery makers benefit from abundant, cheap nickel.

losers.rationale: Idled and deferred non-Indonesian operations keep bleeding.

winners.rationale: Indonesia consolidates a dominant, policy-shaped position in the market.
Battery makers gain a secure, large-volume supply base.

losers.rationale: Producers outside Indonesia struggle to compete structurally on cost.

winners.rationale: Energy-transition demand against tight supply supports producer margins.
Chile and Peru benefit from firm export revenue.

losers.rationale: Grid, construction and EV makers face a rising, structural input cost.

winners.rationale: A widening supply gap keeps pricing power with established producers.
A liquid, trending market rewards positioned investors.

losers.rationale: Electrification buyers absorb sustained cost inflation.

winners.rationale: If new supply stays constrained, the electrification era favours copper producers for years.
Copper-rich states gain durable strategic and fiscal leverage.

losers.rationale: Long-run cost pressure unless substitution (aluminium) or recycling scales.

winners.rationale: China's export licensing gives it pricing leverage over the anode supply.
Western governments funding alternatives gain strategic ground.

losers.rationale: Battery makers outside China face supply uncertainty and licensing delay.

winners.rationale: Non-Chinese graphite developers gain as buyers seek diversification.

losers.rationale: Anode supply remains exposed to policy risk until alternatives mature.

winners.rationale: Synthetic graphite and diversified natural supply reduce buyers' China dependence.
Friend-shoring investment pays off as a resilient supply base.

losers.rationale: China's licensing leverage erodes as the world builds around it.

winners.rationale: China's REE groups and export policy hold decisive near-term leverage.

losers.rationale: Magnet and EV-motor makers face supply and price uncertainty.

winners.rationale: Western stockpiling and financed processing begin to bear fruit.
Non-Chinese producers (MP, Lynas) gain as buyers diversify.

losers.rationale: Magnet supply stays policy-exposed through the transition.

winners.rationale: A diversified magnet supply chain finally lowers buyer risk.
Established non-Chinese producers hold durable share.

losers.rationale: China's structural dominance erodes as alternatives scale — though slowly.

winners.rationale: Heavy-REE supply is the tightest chokepoint; China's leverage is at its peak.

losers.rationale: High-temperature-magnet makers are acutely exposed to export controls.

winners.rationale: Stockpiles and the Malaysia heavy-REE chain offer the first real alternatives.

losers.rationale: Heavy-REE alternatives mature slower than light-REE ones; risk persists.

winners.rationale: Whoever builds non-Chinese heavy-REE separation captures durable, scarce value.
Strategic resilience finally achieved on the hardest chokepoint.

losers.rationale: China's heavy-REE monopoly weakens last, but it does weaken.

winners.rationale: China holds near-total leverage; export policy directly sets the terms.
Licensing-driven scarcity creates wide spreads for those who can source metal.

losers.rationale: GaN/GaAs chip and defense makers face a weaponised, uncertain supply.

winners.rationale: Western funding for alternative gallium recovery begins to reduce dependence.

losers.rationale: Inventory buffers tie up capital; supply stays policy-hostage.

winners.rationale: Recovered and recycled gallium plus new capacity restore supply security.
Diversification investment pays off strategically.

losers.rationale: China's 98% chokehold is a wasting asset once the world builds around it.

winners.rationale: Same as gallium: byproduct concentration plus licensing equals near-term leverage.

losers.rationale: Fiber-optic, infrared and satellite-cell makers face acute supply risk.

winners.rationale: Diversified miners and recyclers (e.g. zinc-stream operators) gain as buyers seek alternatives.

losers.rationale: Specialty-optics supply stays exposed until recovery capacity scales.

winners.rationale: Recycling and diversified byproduct recovery rebuild supply resilience.
Strategic stockpiles and allied supply blunt the chokepoint.

losers.rationale: China's germanium leverage decays as alternatives come online.

winners.rationale: Concentrated producers (Indonesia, China, Myanmar) hold supply leverage.
A scattered supply base creates arbitrage for traders.

losers.rationale: Electronics-solder buyers face a firm price with no easy substitute.

winners.rationale: Non-Chinese tin producers gain as electronics demand holds.

losers.rationale: Solder demand keeps tin costs structurally embedded in electronics.

winners.rationale: Substitution research and tighter solder use slowly reduce exposure.

losers.rationale: Scattered, partly informal supply makes any one state's leverage fragile.
